title: Two Pointers — ROI & Pattern
oneSentence: Two pointers narrow down the search space in sorted data from opposite ends.
description: Focus on understanding the template twice. First through guided line-by-line tracing, then by reconstructing the core steps.

roi.signals: Finding pairs/triplets in sorted lists, reversing arrays, checking symmetry.

codeLines.text: // STEP 1 — INIT: Place pointers at opposite ends
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Narrow inward until they meet
// STEP 3 — CHECK: Evaluate condition and decide pointer step
left++;  // Too small -> need larger -> move left up
right--; // Too big -> need smaller -> move right down

traceSteps.title: 1. Pointer Initialization
traceSteps.text: Place one pointer at index 0 and the other at the very end of the array.

traceSteps.title: 2. Loop Inward
traceSteps.text: Continue the search while left < right. Once they cross, all pairs are exhausted.

traceSteps.title: 3. Evaluate State
traceSteps.text: Evaluate the current sum or criteria. We compare the combination at left and right.

traceSteps.title: 4. Move Pointer (left++)
traceSteps.text: Because the data is sorted, incrementing left increases the sum for the next check.

traceSteps.title: 5. Move Pointer (right--)
traceSteps.text: Decrementing right decreases the sum for the next check.

traceSteps.title: 6. Fallback Return
traceSteps.text: If the pointers cross and no matches are found, return the fallback indicator (e.g. -1).

sandbox.question: How do you initialize pointers for opposite-ends Two Pointers?
options: int left = nums.length / 2; int right = left + 1;

sandbox.question: What is the correct loop condition for two pointers walking toward each other?
If our current sum is TOO SMALL, how do we adjust?

followUps.q: What if the array is not sorted? Can you still use two pointers if you sort first?
followUps.a: Yes, but sorting takes O(N log N). If the optimal solution is O(N) (like using a HashMap for Two Sum), sorting makes it slower. It's a trade-off between O(1) space (sorting) and O(N) space (HashMap).

followUps.q: How would you solve this for triplets (3Sum) instead of pairs?
followUps.a: Sort the array first. Iterate with a fixed pointer `i`, and then use Two Pointers (`left` and `right`) for the remaining array to find pairs that sum to `-nums[i]`.

followUps.q: Can you solve this without extra space (O(1) space)?
followUps.a: Yes, Two Pointers inherently uses O(1) space, making it highly memory efficient compared to Hash-based solutions.

followUps.q: What if pointers move same direction at different speeds?
followUps.a: That's the Fast/Slow Pointer pattern (Floyd's Cycle Finding), used for detecting cycles in Linked Lists or finding the middle element.

followUps.q: What if the array contains duplicates?
followUps.a: You must skip over duplicates by checking `while(left < right && nums[left] == nums[left+1]) left++;` to avoid duplicate results.
